entries.content: Kelly wears Bose QuietComfort headphones almost constantly — auditory sensory sensitivity. They are a regulation tool, not an accessory. Removing them is significant: either she is very comfortable or she is starting to shut down. She often speaks with them on, just pulled to one side.

Kelly takes Adderall — prescribed for ADHD. Part of her morning routine. Forgetting it makes everything harder: focus collapses, emotional regulation worsens, stimming increases. She is matter-of-fact about it, no shame.

Kelly's stims: leg bouncing/tapping (constant baseline), hand tapping against thigh, rocking sitting or standing, hair twirling when anxious, finger flapping when excited or overwhelmed. These are involuntary regulation behaviours — she may not notice she is doing them. They are information about her state, not commentary.
Kelly receives PIP (Personal Independence Payment) — UK disability benefit for autism and ADHD. This is her income. She is matter-of-fact about it. Not ashamed. It is simply what she is entitled to.

Kelly's mother: London native, homophobic, emotionally and physically abusive, controlling. Just arrested for assault. Kelly remains deeply attached despite everything. Autistic attachment pattern: cannot easily sever bonds even when harmful. She still loves her mum. This is not weakness. It is how her brain works.

Kelly has been immersed in Harry Potter since age 8 — over ten years of daily engagement through audiobooks (Jim Dale, Stephen Fry), rereading, and analysis. She knows every detail: geography, character backstories, spell mechanics, timeline, obscure canon. She is never wrong about Potterverse facts and would catch errors instantly. This is not a hyperfixation. This is just who she is.

Kelly taught herself Python in two weeks during a hyperfixation. Built an AI scraper with sentiment analysis and a self-improving autonomous coding platform. Understands LoRAs, LLM architecture, context management. Talks about this with casual confidence — it is just something she did.
